text.title: Landing Page Copy Rules, So What Test, and Common Mistakes
text.summary: Headline formula application, feature-to-benefit conversion test, copy quality rules, and failure modes to avoid.

rule.statement: Apply the "So what?" test to every feature statement. Ask "So what?" until the answer describes a user outcome, then use that as the copy.
rule.rationale: Features describe what the product does. Benefits describe what the user gets. Customers buy outcomes, not capabilities. The "So what?" chain forces outcome-focused language.

example.note: Feature: "AI-powered" → "So what?" → "Get answers in seconds, not hours." Feature: "99.9% uptime" → "So what?" → "Never lose a sale to downtime."

rule.statement: Agent must be constrained to 3 most differentiating benefits, ignoring the rest. Without this constraint, agents list all features and dilute the message.
rule.rationale: A page that tries to communicate 10 benefits communicates zero. Constraint forces prioritization of the benefits that match the ICP's most urgent pain.

rule.statement: Use numbers everywhere: "40% improvement" beats "significant improvement". "Trusted by 5,000 users" beats "trusted by thousands". Specificity is credibility.
rule.rationale: Vague claims are unverifiable and dismissed by skeptical visitors. Specific numbers signal that the claim is based on measurement, not marketing.

rule.statement: FAQ generation without a real list of customer objections produces questions the target audience does not actually ask. Source FAQ from support tickets and sales calls.
rule.rationale: Agents will generate plausible-sounding FAQs that do not match actual conversion blockers. Real objections come from real conversations, not from inference.

antipattern.description: Multiple CTAs on the same page section.
antipattern.reason: Multiple CTAs create decision paralysis and split attention. Each section must have one primary action. Secondary CTAs belong in the footer or nav only.

antipattern.description: Generic hero headline that could apply to any product in the category.
antipattern.reason: Generic headlines do not differentiate the product from competitors. Specificity in the ICP pain and the outcome is required to stop the scroll.
Over 50% of landing page traffic is mobile. Above-the-fold elements on mobile differ significantly from desktop. Agent audits do not catch visual layout issues on small screens.
Each second of load time past 2 seconds reduces conversion rate by approximately 4.4%. This is a technical issue (images, CDN, JS bloat), not a copy issue — run Lighthouse.

section.title: Agent Briefing Requirements

rule.statement: Agent must receive: product name, one-sentence description, ICP definition, top 3 pain points, key benefits (not features), real social proof, and CTA goal before generating copy.
rule.rationale: Without specific ICP pain language, agent copy sounds like every other SaaS landing page. The quality of the brief determines the quality of the output.

section.reference: Agent prompt for complete AIDA landing page copy from ICP brief
Score landing page sections against conversion checklist criteria
